sessions.description:
Grab some lunch from All American Grub. You can even order ahead! Check out the menu here.

Follow the signs on Level 1 near Ballroom F.

sessions.session_title: Lunch Food Truck (Saturday)
sessions.description:
Grab some lunch from Bay Area Bistro. Check out the menu here.

Follow the signs on Level 1 near Ballroom F.

speakers.bio: Alejandra Arevalo is a data analyst at Google Trends. Previously, as a developer fellow at Hearst Newspapers, she built data, AI and visual storytelling products for local newsrooms across the U.S. She also worked at analytics company Chartmetric, exploring music industry trends through data. An active member of the data visualization community, Alejandra's work has been featured in Nightingale, the journal of the Data Visualization Society.

Peyton Barish is the assistant project manager for the Knight-Newhouse College Athletics Database and a former Division I athlete at NC State University. He has a background in college athletics policy with a particular interest in labor policy and innovative sport solutions.

Leah Becerra is the News Media Help Desk Product Director at Reynolds Journalism Institute. She has more than a decade of experience working in local newsrooms across the Midwest and East Coast. She also teaches the Missouri School of Journalism's web design class, where students learn HTML, CSS — a little javascript — and how to use product thinking strategies to build intentional digital experiences for audiences.

Rahul Bhargava (he/him/his) is a researcher, educator, designer, and artist working on creative data storytelling and computational journalism. He creates data murals and theatre with communities, award-winning museum exhibits, AI-powered civic technologies with CSOs, and delivers hands-on workshops and keynote talks across the globe. Rahul’s first book, “Community Data: Creative Approaches to Empowering People with Information”, is now available.

Rae Ellen Bichell is a correspondent with KFF Health News and is based in Colorado. Her recent data work has focused on medical debt, federal grant cuts, Medicaid enrollment and doctor shortages. She used to be an on-air science journalist with NPR and a regional reporter with the Mountain West News Bureau. One time, she started a radio show in Helsinki.

Alexander Borodikhin is a reporter and editor at Mediazona, an independent Russian outlet covering political repression and the war in Ukraine. He heads the English-language site and reports for the data team. Mediazona, in collaboration with the BBC and a team of volunteers, has built a database of 200,000 verified Russian military casualties, data the Kremlin seeks to hide. The team has also scraped Russian courts’ websites to create a full searchable database.

Declan Bradley is a senior at Oberlin College. As a 2025 Dow Jones News Fund intern in data reporting, he developed investigative stories for the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism at the University of Maryland. He is a graduate of the Lede Program for Data Journalism at Columbia University and former intern for The Chronicle of Higher Education. In 2024, he was named Reporter of the Year by the Associated Collegiate Press.

AmyJo Brown is a veteran journalist with a background in local government and investigative reporting. As a consulting editor, she focuses on the organizational challenges specific to doing good journalism at the local level. She designed The Public Ledger — a strategy rooted in beat expertise and hard-won knowledge of newsroom economics — to transform public records into research infrastructure, helping newsrooms track money, map power & surface story leads faster.

Silvia Canelón (she/her/ella) is a data journalist, R educator and researcher with a love for Philly and a passion for data literacy. She values creative ways of using data to connect individual stories to broader patterns that impact entire communities. Silvia would love to meet other data journalists, so please say hello! Lately, her favorite topics of conversation include open source tools, AI skepticism, psychological thrillers and pottery.

Emma Carew Grovum is the founder of Kimbap Media, working at the intersection of journalism, technology and audience. She holds a Cornell University certificate in Building a Diverse Workforce, completed leadership programs at Yale and Poynter, and participated in CUNY’s 2025 AI Lab for Leaders. Emma has previously worked at The Marshall Project, The Daily Beast, The New York Times, Foreign Policy, The Chronicle of Philanthropy and The Star Tribune.

Matt Carroll is a journalism professor at Northeastern University. He worked for 26 years at the Boston Globe, specializing in data storytelling. He was a member of the Spotlight team, the newsroom’s investigative unit, when it won the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service in 2003 for its coverage of the Catholic priest sexual abuse scandal. That story was turned into the movie “Spotlight,” which won the Oscar for Best Picture.

Jill Castellano is a data journalist for The Marshall Project. She has worked for ABC Television, inewsource, The Desert Sun and The Salt Lake Tribune. Along with her colleagues, she was awarded the Pulitzer Prize for explanatory reporting in 2018 for her work with USA Today on the deaths of undocumented border crossers.

speakers.affiliation: Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project
speakers.bio: Ezana Ćeman is the Product Manager for data platforms at OCCRP, where she oversees the development of tools like OCCRP Aleph Pro. Since 2021, she has focused on user-centered innovation to support the organization’s strategic goals. Ezana holds a master’s in IT and is a Grace Hopper and two-time Forbes 30 Under 30 Scholar. Additionally, she leads digital education and advocacy at FemLab, an NGO advancing gender equality in Bosnia and Herzegovina.
Aisvarya is a computational journalist at the Tow Center for Digital Journalism, where she studies the uses and impact of AI tools in journalism.

Carl Churchill is a graphics reporter with The Wall Street Journal. He works primarily on issues relating to climate, ecology and U.S. news. He joined the Journal after working for Woodwell Climate Research Center, a climate science organization based in Massachusetts. While there, he received recognition for his graphics work on Amazon deforestation and Arctic climate change. He has a master's in GIS from Arizona State University.

Laine Cibulskis is a senior at the University of Missouri studying journalism and economics. In addition to reporting for the Columbia Missourian, Laine assists investigative journalists working on books through the Watchdog Writers Group. She has previously covered international trade in Brussels, personal finance in Washington, D.C. and business in Missouri.

Mónica Cordero is an Ortega y Gasset Award-winning investigative journalist and recipient of the 2026 A-Mark Prize for Investigative Reporting. She reports on agriculture, global trade and environmental policy for Investigative Midwest and the Mississippi River Basin Ag & Water Desk, blending collaborative investigations with data-driven analysis. Her work has appeared in The New York Times, openDemocracy, Univision and Radio Ambulante.

Andrew Couts is Senior Editor, Security & Investigations at WIRED, overseeing cybersecurity, privacy, policy, national security, and surveillance coverage. He also oversees investigations across WIRED's newsroom.
Will Craft is a data editor at the Guardian.

David Cuillier is director of the Brechner Freedom of Information Project at the University of Florida College of Journalism and Communications. He is a former journalist and taught journalism at the University of Arizona for 17 years. He is former president of the Society of Professional Journalists and the National Freedom of Information Coalition and co-author of "The Art of Access: Strategies for Acquiring Public Records."

speakers.affiliation: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP)

speakers.bio: Jovi Dai is an interactive visualization developer at the National Association of REALTORS®, where he develops charts and other visualizations that turn complex data into clear, accessible information for readers. Before joining NAR, he was a data reporter for Bay Area News Group, producing data-driven and visual stories for the San Jose Mercury News and East Bay Times focused on the San Francisco Bay Area and issues affecting local communities.

Becky is a data journalist specialising in environmental storytelling, investigation and data visualisation. She loves spatial analysis and the technical challenges of interrogating really big datasets.

Nick is a senior data editor on ESPN’s visual storytelling team. He has worked in coding and journalism for the last 13 years, most recently as an investigative data reporter at CBS News. Originally from Monmouth, Illinois, he is based in Philadelphia.

Claudia is a second-year grad student in journalism, after graduating with a B.S.J. in May 2024. She is from Shepherdstown, West Virginia, and has worked many internships and part-time jobs as a multimedia journalist. She has received several awards for her reporting, including a ninth-place Hearst Award for television news and a third-place BEA of Festival Media Arts for a hard news story. Claudia loves her state and hopes to continue bringing it to life.

Amy DiPierro is a data journalist soon to join The War Horse, an award-winning nonprofit newsroom that reports on the U.S. military and veterans issues. She recently covered colleges and universities for EdSource, California’s largest education newsroom. She was previously a data journalist at the Center for Public Integrity.

speakers.affiliation: Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project
speakers.bio: Klil is the head of engineering for data platforms at the Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project. He leads a team of developers and data engineers creating data-intensive, secure, cloud-native platforms for investigative research. Klil's background spans software development, cybersecurity and nonprofit technology strategy. He has worked on mission-driven sectors ranging from risk management, AML, physical security, journalism and disinformation.

Angela Eichhorst is CT Mirror's first AI data reporter / product developer, where she is focused on developing internal newsroom tools for investigative reporting and data analysis for stories. Previously she was a student journalist, fact-checked at CNN, and produced polls at the AP-NORC Center, including VoteCast. In her free time, Angela enjoys exploring Connecticut and playing boardgames.

speakers.affiliation: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP)

speakers.bio: Sandra Fish is a kinda retired data journalist specializing in politics. She's worked with the Colorado Sun, New Mexico In Depth and other news outlets, and she was a journalism instructor at the University of Colorado Boulder. In 2026, she's helping newsrooms implement QuizTheVote.
Hannah Fresques is deputy data editor at ProPublica, where she has edited data-driven investigations on the aftermath of Texas’s abortion ban, high-interest tribal lending and a Salmonella outbreak.

Inaara Gangji is a freelance journalist who has worked in Africa, the U.S. and the Middle East. She studied Journalism at Northwestern University and her work has appeared in the AP, Forbes, USA Today and Al Jazeera, centering on women, data, and social issues. She is currently finishing her Master's in Journalism at UC Berkeley and has been an Overseas Press Club of America Fellow, a Rosalynn Carter Journalism Fellow, and a White House Correspondents Association Scholar.

Adrian D. Garcia has been the managing editor of data visualizations for Financial Times Specialist since January 2024. His team covers topics related to corporate boards, asset and wealth management and insurance. In the fall, he teaches an intro to data journalism course at the Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY. He also serves on the NAHJ Business Journalism Task Force.
Software engineer building open source tools for newsrooms and journalists! Based in Los Angeles, working on Datasette, campaign finance tools, SQLite tooling, data visualizations, and more!

Juan Pablo Garnham is communication and policy engagement manager for the Eviction Lab. He is a reporter with experience covering housing, homelessness and urban issues across the United States and Latin America. His work has been featured by news outlets like The Texas Tribune, The Guardian, Bloomberg and Univision.
Elisabeth is a data journalist with American Public Media and a visiting journalist-in-residence at Indiana University.

Caroline Ghisolfi is the deputy data editor for the Houston Chronicle. Before coming to Houston, Caroline was at the Austin American-Statesman, where she was part of the team selected as a Pulitzer finalist in public service. She trained in data journalism in multiple roles at the Associated Press and holds a journalism master's degree from Stanford University.

Jack Gillum is a reporter in The Wall Street Journal's Washington bureau, where he focuses on data-driven stories about politics and government. Jack has worked at Bloomberg News, ProPublica, the Washington Post, the Associated Press and USA Today. He has broken stories that included the existence and location of Hillary Clinton’s private email server, a U.S.-backed "Cuban Twitter" program and standardized test cheating in the nation's capital.

Brendan is director of insights at OpenSecrets. He started working in the money in politics space with the Campaign Finance Institute in 2001. He has worked extensively with congressional and presidential campaign donor data, modeling public financing proposals, and outside spending. He also worked on creating CFI’s database of state laws. Brendan graduated from Colgate University with degrees in political science and history.

Susanna Granieri is a reporter and researcher for First Amendment Watch. She previously reported on politics and policy for the Albany-based Legislative Gazette and investigated the use of faulty forensic science in death penalty convictions with the Mississippi Center for Investigative Reporting. She is a graduate of SUNY New Paltz and the Columbia Journalism School.

Clayton Guse is the transportation and infrastructure editor at New York Public Radio, where he oversees coverage of pressing issues facing the country's largest city. He previously served as the transit reporter for the New York Daily News, leading coverage of mass transit in the city throughout the COVID-19 pandemic.

Holly is data editor at KFF Health News, a national nonprofit newsroom dedicated to health policy and health care coverage. Previously, she was an investigative reporter at The Dallas Morning News. She lives in Maryland.

Jasmine Ye Han is a news graphics developer at Informa TechTarget (Industry Dive), where she tells business news stories with data analysis and visualizations. Previously she was a data journalism reporter at Bloomberg Industry Group. Jasmine is an alumnus of the Missouri School of Journalism and NICAR data library. She moved to the U.S. from China in 2014.
Hargarten is a Minneapolis-based journalist at the intersection of data analysis, reporting, coding and design.

Jennefer Harper is the co-founder of Decoherence Media, a nonprofit investigative media outlet. Her work focuses on far-right extremism in the US and transnational networks. She approaches her research and reporting through an intersectional framework using open and closed sources. She has a special interest in accelerationism, youth radicalization and government misconduct. In 2025, her work on a written investigation with The New Arab was shortlisted for an Amnesty Media Award.

Oliver Hawkins is a data scientist in the FT's visual and data journalism team. He has previously worked as a data scientist and a statistical researcher for the UK Parliament’s research service, and as a data journalist for the BBC. Oliver built the data analysis pipeline for the FT’s live coverage of US election results in 2024. Since then he has worked for the computational investigations team, specialising in analysis of blockchain data.

Hannah is a second-year, 4+1 grad student pursuing a career in reporting. She graduates in May 2026 with a bachelors and masters degree in journalism. She’s from Morgantown, West Virginia, and has worked as a news reporter and opinion editor for The Daily Athenaeum. While she completes her masters, she also does freelance writing for local magazines, including GoXplore and the Tri-State Business Journal.
Eade Hemingway is a computational journalist at the FT's visual and data journalism team. She works on data-driven stories, using coding to collate, analyse and present datasets.

Destiny Herbers is an investigative journalist with the Flatwater Free Press in Nebraska, where she uses data to write about everything from agricultural land sales to the toll of the opioid epidemic. She got started in journalism as a fellow of the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism at the University of Maryland.

Tabitha Hidalgo is an investigative-minded  journalist and incoming May 2026 graduate of the University of North Texas. She has led student newsrooms as editor-in-chief of the North Texas Daily and San Antonio College’s Sundial, and currently serves as president of UNT’s NAHJ chapter. She is passionate about storytelling that elevates underrepresented voices.

Mia is a senior at Indiana University Bloomington studying journalism. She is co-editor-in-chief of the Indiana Daily Student and a board member and intern for the Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism.

Josh Hinkle is KXAN’s director of investigations and innovation, leading the Austin, Texas, station’s 13-person investigative team on multiple platforms. He also leads KXAN’s political coverage as the executive producer and host of “State of Texas,” a weekly program focused on the Texas Legislature and elections, seen in 14 markets statewide. Josh also serves as IRE Board President.

Emily Hopkins is a reporter at Mirror Indy focused on data and accountability. Recent work includes an investigation into ethical issues within Indianapolis Mayor Joe Hogsett's administration and an investigation into the Indianapolis Housing Agency.

speakers.affiliation: Reveal from The Center for Investigative Reporting

speakers.bio: Developer Experience Engineer Sanjin Ibrahimovic is dedicated to building and supporting our developer community while enhancing the end-user and developer experience across MuckRock's offerings. His work includes writing documentation, drafting release notes, handling developer communications, developing new add-ons and improving MuckRock's APIs. He graduated cum laude in Computer Science from the Illinois Institute of Technology

Julia Ingram is a data journalist at CBS News in New York. She is a part of CBS News's Confirmed team, which focuses on visual verification, fact-checking and open-source intelligence. Lately, her reporting has centered on immigration and the Trump administration's deportation crackdown.

Caresse is an award-winning national consumer investigative reporter based in Washington D.C. with Gray Media’s InvestigateTV. Prior to InvestigateTV, she was a consumer investigative reporter at WSMV-TV in Nashville, TN. She has prior reporting experience at WWL-TV in New Orleans, WJRT in Flint, Michigan, and at WCBI in Columbus, Mississippi. She started her career behind the scenes as a producer at WJTV in Jackson, Mississippi.

Sahana Jayaraman is an award-winning data reporter at The Banner. She is responsible for both analyses that provide context to quick-turn stories and those that build the backbone of enterprise and investigative projects. Previously, she worked at The Arizona Republic and The Washington Post. She primarily works in R and Python.

Jaclyn Jeffrey-Wilensky is a data reporter for The Wall Street Journal. Before coming to The Journal, Jaclyn reported data-driven web and radio stories for WNYC/Gothamist, where her work on gun violence and COVID-19 won awards from the New York Press Club and the Deadline Club of New York. Jaclyn is an alumna of Cornell University and of the Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY, where she now teaches data journalism classes.

Brent has spent his career in nonprofit news, starting as a member of the founding cohort of the St. Louis Beacon in 2008, joining St. Louis Public Radio in 2013 and most recently joining the editorial graphics team at NPR in 2023. He enjoys building tools and automations to help the team and wider newsroom. Outside work, Brent enjoys photography, singing and doing the daily New York Times crossword puzzle. He's based in the metro St. Louis area.

Allie Kanik is data editor for the Houston Chronicle. She is a player coach, specializing in data visualization, mapping and Python analysis. Allie previously worked as a data reporter at Louisville Public Media in Kentucky and PublicSource in Pittsburgh.

Adiel Kaplan is the program director for the Tow-Knight Center for Journalism Futures, where she focuses on how journalism can respond to and thrive as artificial intelligence disrupts news production, dissemination and consumption. She has worked as an investigative reporter and editor at local and national news outlets, including NBC News and the Miami Herald, and teaches investigative reporting for the investigative and data programs at Columbia Journalism School.
Keegan is a data reporter covering AI and tech for Sherwood News. Previously, he was at The Markup and The Wall Street Journal.

Emily is the founding director of the Centre for Anthropology and Journalism. Previously she was a senior editor and magazine editor-in-chief. She is a trained anthropologist (University of Victoria) and holds a Masters in Journalism from Carleton University, where she has also worked as an instructor.

Stacy Kess is the founder and chief of editorial of Equal Access Public Media (EAPM). She is a reporter and editor with more than 25 years’ experience and has contended with life-long health issues. She is also a survivor of a head-on car-crash in 2017. She credits this as the beginning of EAPM. Kess said this led her to her mission of decreasing the accessibility barriers in the news. Kess oversees editorial and media at EAPM. She currently lives in Boston, Massachusetts.

Sam Kessler is a reporter for The Wall Street Journal based in New York City. He focuses on the intersection of power, business and technology, using data analysis and programming alongside traditional reporting to uncover investigative stories. Previously, Sam covered the crypto industry as deputy managing editor at CoinDesk, where he was part of a team that won a 2023 Gerald Loeb Award for its coverage of Sam Bankman-Fried and FTX.

Maheen Khan is the director of technology at the Invisible Institute. She studied information analysis and computer science in undergrad, and now works to maintain the Invisible Institute's tech tools (like CPDP) and support with data analysis for investigations.

Haohsiang is a data journalist on the FT’s visual and data journalism team based in Hong Kong, covering Asia news stories through data analysis and graphics. Before joining the FT, he led the data journalism team at The Reporter in Taiwan, collaborating with journalists on investigative and in-depth stories.

Jared Kofsky produces investigative and breaking news stories for ABC News platforms, including Good Morning America, World News Tonight with David Muir, ABC News Live, This Week with George Stephanopoulos, Nightline and ABCNews.com, working from both the field and the network’s headquarters in New York City. He is also an on-air contributor for ABC News Radio. Jared previously covered news in South Carolina and New Jersey.

Karina Kumar is a data reporter at the Austin American-Statesman. Most recently, she has used data and records to report on local government and compensation with a focus on accountability. She enjoys finding ways to make data accessible and easy to digest for readers.

A.J. Lagoe is an investigative reporter for KARE-TV in Minneapolis. His reporting routinely leads to criminal convictions, legislative hearings, and new state and federal laws. A.J. is a multi-time recipient of journalism’s highest honors including Peabody, Polk, duPont Columbia and IRE awards.

Stephanie Lamm is a data reporter at the Atlanta Journal-Constitution who uses public records, data analysis and shoe-leather reporting to hold institutions accountable and uncover information that would otherwise remain hidden. She graduated from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and spent five years working in Texas before landing in Atlanta.

Allan Lasser has over a decade of experience in digital publishing and software design. As MuckRock's head of product, he works across departments to provide design direction, manage product roadmaps, listen to user feedback and identify new opportunities to help journalists do their best work.
Koby Levin is a senior reporter for Outlier Media. He lives in Detroit.

George LeVines is the editor of the Gun Violence Data Hub, a project of The Trace. The Trace is a nonprofit newsroom that exclusively covers gun violence in the U.S. He formerly worked at the Los Angeles Times, NPR and CQ Roll Call.

Ryan Little is a deputy data editor at ProPublica, where he helps lead a team of data reporters on investigative and accountability projects. Prior to joining ProPublica, he was the data editor at The Baltimore Banner. Stories he has worked on have won multiple national awards, including the 2025 Philip Meyer. He is a frequent speaker on data journalism and a dedicated mentor to emerging data reporters.

Liz Lucas is the Houston Harte Chair in Journalism at Mizzou, where she teaches courses in data journalism and emerging technologies. She previously worked as a data reporter and editor for various newsrooms and as a trainer for Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE).

Clay Ludwig is a journalism student at the University of Maryland who develops AI-powered tools for reporters. When he isn't talking to his favorite LLMs, he covers science and technology for The Diamondback, the university's independent student newspaper, and builds apps on the side. NICAR 2026 will be his first time at the conference as both an attendee and a speaker.

Craig Lyons joined the Arnolt Center in November 2021 as its first managing editor. Lyons has spent nearly 20 years covering government agencies at the local, state and federal level in Michigan, Indiana, New Hampshire, Vermont and Maine. Lyons’ work has earned recognition from the Chicago Headline Club, Michigan Press Association, Hoosier State Press Association and the Indiana Chapter of the Society of Professional Journalists.

Tory Lysik is a media research fellow and journalist fellow at the Tow Center for Digital Journalism at Columbia University, reporting on AI, misinformation, higher education and politics. Based in New York City, she was previously a data and graphics journalist at Axios, reporting interactive data visualizations for national audiences, and a reporter at Vox and Poynter Institute in Washington, D.C. Her work has appeared in The Marshall Project, Slate and others.

Michelle Ma is a reporter for Bloomberg News in Los Angeles. She covers clean energy and climate technologies. She also writes about the climate impacts of the AI data center boom and the intersection of climate change, electric utilities and insurance in the western US. Previously, she was a climate reporter at Protocol. Before that, she was a news editor of live journalism and special coverage at The Wall Street Journal.

Tyler Machado is a freelance web developer and design technologist, located in Boston, MA. Tyler has created data-driven visual stories and dashboards for publications like the Pudding, Science News, Harvard Business Review and Northeastern University. He has also worked on design systems, UX/UI and frontend development for independent media websites across in-house and agency roles, and recently designed the branding for Boston’s new NWSL soccer supporters’ group.

Prinz has been a graphics reporter for Reuters since 2022, designing charts and other visuals to better tell stories behind financial and economic data. Prior to this, he was based in the Philippines, where he worked as an economic beat journalist for over a decade covering macroeconomy, public finance and the financial markets for various local and international outlets. His favorite chart -- and breakfast-- is a waffle.

speakers.affiliation: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington
speakers.bio: Robert is the vice president for research and data at Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW), where he leads efforts to investigate dark money and corruption. Prior to joining CREW, he founded the dark money tracking program at OpenSecrets, where he won SPJ’s Sigma Delta Chi award for his reporting. He has collaborated with and appeared in major outlets nationwide like NPR, The Washington Post, the New York Times and CNN.

Olivia Mapes is the editor-in-chief of The Exponent, Purdue's independent newspaper. Mapes is a senior studying creative writing and literature and planning on becoming a full-time journalist. During her time at The Exponent she has broken the news on the restructuring of the honors college, which the university has still not publicly announced, and did a deep dive on the effects of an insurance change on university faculty.

Arlene Martínez is with Good Jobs First, which promotes corporate and government accountability in economic development. GJF provides extensive research and technical assistance to policymakers, journalists and others studying the effectiveness of taxpayer subsidies for “economic development” as well as corporate behavior. Arlene previously reported at outlets including Hispanic Link News Service, the Los Angeles Times, The (Allentown) Morning Call and the USA TODAY Network.

Adam Marton is a data journalist, senior lecturer at The Philip Merrill College of Journalism at The University of Maryland and director of the Capital News Service Data & Graphics bureau. He previously worked at The Baltimore Sun as the senior editor of data and graphics. His work on police accountability has twice been named as a Pulitzer Prize finalist: in 2024 for Lethal Restraint and in 2015 for the 45 Minute Mystery of Freddie Gray’s Death.

Ty McClung is a second-year graduate student from Madison, West Virginia, who is pursuing a Master’s in Journalism at West Virginia University. He graduated from WVU in May of 2024 with his Bachelor of Science in Journalism with a focus in broadcast television production. Ty is currently serving as the television journalism program graduate assistant. Ty has experience reporting and anchoring in various capacities.

Sean McMinn is editor of data and graphics at POLITICO. His team covers federal policy and politics through data reporting and visualizations. He previously worked at NPR news apps and investigations teams and CQ Roll Call, and has taught classes at American University and Northwestern Medill School of Journalism.

Dhrumil Mehta is a professor of data journalism at Columbia Journalism School and deputy director of the Tow Center for Digital Journalism. He also teaches at the Harvard Kennedy School and previously worked as a database journalist at FiveThirtyEight.

Elena is a senior data visualization reporter and project lead for U.S. elections at Bloomberg News. She leads a team to execute the design and development of U.S. election results projects, collaborates with other reporters to analyze datasets related to election voting patterns and immigration, and creates visualizations for data-driven stories.

Aaron Mendelson is a data journalist at The Trace, a nonprofit newsroom that covers gun violence. He works on The Trace’s Gun Violence Data Hub initiative. Previously, Mendelson was an investigative and data reporter at the Center for Public Integrity and NPR affiliate KPCC/LAist. His reporting has garnered honors including a Gerald Loeb Award, Online Journalism Awards and an IRE Award.
Jeremy Merrill is a data reporter writing primarily about technology at The Washington Post. He lives in Atlanta.

Chris works at the intersection of technology and journalism, and he is an expert at helping bridge the gap between cutting-edge tech and the reporters who rely on it. At Meta, CrowdTangle and Everlaw, he has built and scaled products used by newsrooms worldwide, led complex partnerships and advised journalists and researchers navigating the rapidly evolving information space.

Andrew Miller has served as co-editor-in-chief for the Indiana Daily Student since August 2025. There, he’s previously held the positions of science and environmental reporter, elections reporter, news editor and managing editor. In summer 2024, he interned at The Oregonian in Portland, Oregon, reporting on business and the timber industry. In summer last year, he reported general assignment at The Herald-Times.

Charles Minshew is the senior editor of data journalism for The Atlanta Journal-Constitution, where he leads a team of reporters using data, open records and code to tell stories about Atlanta, Georgia and the South. He also serves on the board of the Georgia First Amendment Foundation.

An award-winning teacher, Elaine Monaghan is a professor of practice in journalism at Indiana University-Bloomington. Elaine spent 20 years in the field, mostly as a foreign correspondent for Reuters in Moscow, Kyiv, Dublin, Kosovo and Washington. Elaine has blogged about the election of President Barack Obama for Microsoft UK, worked for Congressional Quarterly and CQ magazine, and was a Washington correspondent for The Times of London, where she also authored a column, Abroad in America.
Aaron Mondry is a senior reporter with Outlier Media who covers stories about housing, real estate and development in Detroit.
Dylan is a cartographer and graphics reporter at the Washington Post. He makes damn good pies.

Greg Morton is the data editor at the The Banner, where he leads a team of data journalists dedicated to pursuing ambitious lines of reporting, answering important questions and producing impactful journalism that informs and challenges readers.

Laura Moscoso is a journalist and educator with 15 years of experience, currently living in Manatí, Puerto Rico. She has worked in small nonprofits, national publications, and both public and private universities. Laura joined the IRE training team in 2022, where she leads custom training, the Spanish training program, and collaboration efforts with sister organizations.

Christopher (Chris) Mullin, Ph.D., is strategy director of data and measurement for Lumina Foundation, an independent, private foundation in Indianapolis that is committed to making opportunities for learning beyond high school available to all. Mullin holds a bachelor’s degree in art education and a doctorate in higher education administration from the University of Florida. He also has a master’s in education from Teachers College, Columbia University.

Maggie Mulvihill is a Boston-based award-winning investigative reporter and journalism teacher who has trained hundreds of journalists and other professionals on how to use data-driven and investigative reporting storytelling techniques and computational journalism methods to produce deeply reported government accountability stories. Last year, she and her students shared in the top prize for IRE's student investigative journalism and serves on IRE’s Academic Task Force.

Sana Muneer is a junior at Boston University studying journalism and psychology. Most recently, she served as the general assignment nightside news co-op at Boston.com. She is currently vice president of BU’s independent student newspaper, The Daily Free Press, and also held roles as managing co-editor and features editor. Her work can be found in The Dorchester Reporter, The Marblehead Current, BU Today, Student Press Law Center and The Stroll Glen Ellyn.

Cat is a second-year master's student and Howard Center for Investigative Journalism fellow at the University of Maryland's Philip Merrill College of Journalism. She graduated from Quinnipiac University with a bachelor's degree in journalism in 2024. Cat's main interests are in data-driven reporting and public records, and she recently collaborated with the Associated Press and NPR as part of a Howard Center investigation into rail safety.
Sean Mussenden is interim director and data editor of the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism, an investigative unit housed in the University of Maryland Philip Merrill College of Journalism.

Justin Myers (any pronouns) is a Chicago-based data journalist, visual journalist, developer and parent. They oversee the graphics team for Chicago Public Media, which includes both the Chicago Sun-Times and NPR member station WBEZ. Justin previously was the data editor at The Associated Press and has been part of data and visuals teams for several other newsrooms. They hold degrees in journalism and engineering, and they deeply appreciate a good nap.

Adriana Navarro is a data journalist at the Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting, where she covers the intersection of gender, politics and public policy. Previously, Navarro worked on an award-winning investigative series examining privately sponsored travel and lobbying loopholes in Congress. Her work has appeared in The Baltimore Banner, Politico and The Washington Post.

​​Kara is the deputy data editor at UMD's Howard Center for Investigative Journalism. She has 15 years of experience across newspapers, public radio and online outlets and has covered topics ranging from immigration to real estate to education. At her hometown paper in Pennsylvania, Kara’s investigation of a wealthy school district led to board member resignations, a more transparent superintendent search and the restoration of elementary art and music classes.

Duy Nguyen is a data scientist and engineer for The New York Times A.I. Initiatives team, where he leads original research and tooling to unlock new reporting and production capabilities, while maintaining responsible A.I. use. Before that, he helped local media solve business and audience problems with A.I. at Columbia Journalism School. He was previously a data journalist at The Times and NPR and briefly contributed to DocumentCloud.

Mark Nichols is a senior manager of data journalism for ABC News, developing data-driven investigative stories and projects for ABC's World News Tonight, Nightline, Good Morning America, Live, and digital content for abcnews.com. He also works with the data team that serves ABC's Owned Television Station affiliates in eight major U.S. cities.

Taylor is a data reporter covering education and local government for the Houston Chronicle. She holds a master’s degree in data journalism from the University of Maryland, where she worked for the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism. Her reporting has taken her from covering housing in Maine and Washington, D.C. to writing about higher education nationwide. She's passionate about accountability journalism and building tools to make data easy to use and understand.

Audrey Nielsen is a reporter, researcher and data journalist with a focus on public accountability, historical records and public data. Audrey develops reporting tools and resources for collaborative journalism. She is an investigative and research journalist at Muckrock.

Sachita is a PhD candidate at Northwestern University, and her research asks: how can systems for finding, making sense of, and reporting on information support journalists' values and practices? New technologies like generative AI introduce value trade-offs: accuracy vs. efficiency, independence vs. algorithmic support, and so on. In response, she builds AI systems to surface these trade-offs and help journalists navigate them, in support of public-interest journalism.

Gavin Off is a data journalism lecturer at the University of Florida. Prior to joining UF, he spent 15 years as an investigative data reporter at the Charlotte Observer, where his reporting and data analysis helped win several national awards, including an IRE award.

Soo Oh is deputy managing editor of impact at CalMatters and The Markup, where she leads the data and product teams. She has previously edited investigations, reported stories and built data-driven journalism, from scraping and analyzing datasets to coding interactive graphics and developing internal tools.

Caitlin Ostroff is a data reporter for The Wall Street Journal in New York, where she covers crypto, finance and politics. She was previously based in London, covering crypto and European finance for The Journal. She's landed scoops and chronicled turmoil at crypto exchanges FTX and Binance.

Katherine Oung is a data reporter for the Houston Chronicle. They graduated from Vanderbilt University with a degree in Computer Science and Political Science. They previously interned on the Interactive News Technology team at The New York Times and served as the Data Editor of The Vanderbilt Hustler, their college paper.

Paul Overberg is a member of the data team at The Wall Street Journal, where he won an Emmy last year as part of a team that produced a video investigation of Tesla crashes. He worked on USA TODAY’s data team for many years. He also has taught at Northwestern's Medill School of Journalism and served as a senior fellow for the Center for Health Journalism at the University of Southern California.

Haley is a fellow at the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism and a graduate student at the Philip Merrill College of Journalism. She currently reports for Capital News Service, a newswire run by the University of Maryland, and is based in Washington, D.C. She holds a bachelor’s degree from Pomona College.

speakers.affiliation: American Association for the Advancement of Science
speakers.bio: Veronica Penney is a graphics editor and data reporter at Science magazine, where she reports, analyzes data and designs graphics for the newsroom. She specializes in data reporting, with an emphasis on climate change and cartography, and is an instructor for Columbia Journalism School’s Lede Program. Previously, she worked as an investigative data reporter for Colorado Public Radio and as a climate and graphics reporter for The New York Times and The Washington Post.
Scott Pham is a data reporter at CBS News, where he works on original investigations and collaborates across the network. He also serves as the managing editor for the data team.

Cheryl Phillips is a lecturer at Stanford and founder of Big Local News, which supports newsrooms by sharing public data for accountability reporting. Last year, Big Local News contributed to a Pulitzer Prize for Local Reporting with The Baltimore Banner, The New York Times and nine other newsrooms. She spent 12 years at The Seattle Times, where she was a part of two Pulitzers for breaking news and two Pulitzer finalists. She is a former IRE board president.

Lisa Pickoff-White is the California Reporting Project’s director of research, based at Berkeley Journalism's Investigative Reporting Program. She turns police violence and misconduct records into data and impactful reporting with journalists and engineers. At KQED, she worked on award-winning investigations examining fire risk, emergency response breakdowns and jail conditions. She’s also built applications to meet audience needs, from fire maps to election results.
Justin Price uses data and public records to tell stories for the Atlanta Journal-Constitution.

Tara Puckey is the president and CEO of the Radio Television Digital News Association (RTDNA). She has held senior leadership roles at both RTDNA and the Society of Professional Journalists, and she has trained more than 3,000 journalists and professionals in effective leadership. Recognized as one of Association Forum’s "Forty Under 40" and an Obama Leader for 25–26, she is a Certified Association Executive and Fellow of the American Society of Association Executives.
Amy Qin is a data reporter for WBEZ, where she uses a combination of data analysis, computer programming and traditional reporting methods to tell data-driven stories for broadcast and print.

Phoebe Quinton is a data journalist at the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, where she works on the politics and investigative teams. Previously, she worked on a special report at Reuters to investigate how a federal agency became an unlikely ally for companies and others responsible for cleanups at toxic sites.

Kaitlyn Radde is a computational social scientist at Pew Research Center. She previously worked as a data visualization designer at the Center and completed news reporting and graphics internships at NPR and Chalkbeat.

Disha Raychaudhuri is a data journalist at Reuters covering global inequality, race and social justice. She is also an active member of the Reuters Peer Network, offering peer‑to‑peer mental health support to colleagues. Before joining Reuters in 2020, she was a data reporter at The Star‑Ledger, covering demographics, inequality and policing. She moved to the U.S. in 2016 after reporting stints in India and Bangladesh focused on education and oral‑history projects.

Hannah Reale covers children and families in Massachusetts. She’s sampled journalism that touches just one New York neighborhood to every corner of the globe, reporting for NPR, The World, The Wire China, The West Side Rag and The Wesleyan Argus. As an editor, she works with journalists across the newsroom to improve web stories for structure, style and accuracy. She’s a determined newshound, shutterbug and eagle-eyed editor who ferrets out hard-to-wrangle datasets.

Alex Reed is project manager for Mapping Black California and a proud Carengie Mellon University Tartan. A Jane of all trades, she works at the intersection of data and storytelling to make complex information accessible and engaging. Through dynamic visualizations, her work addresses social disparities in education, economic opportunity, public health and environmental rights.

Rob Reid is the Texas Tribune's education data developer, building a K-12 education data warehouse and web tools while supporting reporters with data analysis. He joined in 2024 after a data journalism internship at the Jacksonville Tributary, Columbia University's Lede Program and data consulting for Northwestern's Medill School. In a prior career, he developed a case management database with Healthy Families America.

Mike Reilley is a senior lecturer at UIC in Chicago. He teaches digital, data and AI journalism classes and runs the student lab site RedLineProject.news. He is the founder of JournalistsToolbox.ai and is the author of two books: "Data + Journalism: A Story-Driven Approach to Learning Data Reporting" and "The Journalist's Toolbox: A Guide to Digital Reporting and AI".

Elizabeth Renter leads the Data Studies team at NerdWallet, directing the production of original data journalism and proprietary consumer sentiment surveys. Sitting between the editorial and communications teams, Elizabeth also serves as a primary media spokesperson, translating complex macroeconomic trends into insights for the general public. Her nontraditional path highlights the evolution from data journalist to strategic leadership.

Steven Rich is a data reporter at The New York Times, after having spent more than a decade at The Washington Post. He’s worked on investigations probing policing, immigration, opioids and subjects spanning nearly every beat. He has been a reporter on two teams awarded Pulitzer Prizes, in 2014 and 2016, and on two teams awarded a Peabodys, in 2018 and 2021. He previously served three terms on IRE’s board of directors.

Alex Richards is a journalism professor at Syracuse University’s Newhouse School. Before joining the academy, he was a reporter and editor at news organizations including the Chicago Tribune, The Chronicle of Higher Education and the Las Vegas Sun, as well as the consumer finance company NerdWallet. He's also a former IRE training director.

Jessica Riley is a graduate student receiving both a bachelor's and a master's in journalism from West Virginia University, specializing in digital reporting. She has a strong passion for hard news/investigative work as well as data-driven stories.

Max Rivera is a data journalist. He’s a reporter at Bloomberg News, where he uses code to dig through large datasets and uncover stories across a wide range of beats. His reporting has ranged from immigration and automotive safety to sports.

C.J. Robinson is a reporting and research fellow at the Tow Center for Digital Journalism at Columbia University, where he writes data stories on AI, media and politics. He graduated in 2025 with a M.S. in Data Journalism at Columbia Journalism School, and previously worked as a product analyst on The New York Times Games team.

Cam Rodriguez is a data and investigative reporter based in Chicago, and she most recently was a local investigations fellow at The New York Times. She has worked with teams at MuckRock, Chalkbeat, USA TODAY, South Side Weekly, the Detroit Free Press, WTTW and the Better Government Association, and she writes as much as she codes. When she’s not digging in archives or making another pot of coffee, she’s usually playing with maps, watching rom-coms or exploring the Midwest with her dog.

Simon Rogers is an award-winning data journalist, writer and speaker. He is co-host of The Data Journalism Podcast and author of "Facts are Sacred." He has also written a range of infographics for children books from Candlewick. Rogers is data editor at Google, advisor to the Sigma Data Journalism Awards and author of "What We Ask Google," published in 2026.

Katey Rusch is an investigative reporter at UC Berkeley’s Investigative Reporting Program, focused on police accountability and public records. A Pulitzer Prize finalist, she specializes in data-driven investigations.

Emilia Ruzicka is a data journalist seeking their next full-time job. They work part-time managing data for the Trans News Initiative and were formerly a data reporter at Stacker. They have published with the Urban Institute, the Center for News, Technology, and Innovation, and other outlets. Emilia enjoys reporting on health, science, technology, queer issues and public data. They have an MA from U. of Virginia and a BS from Brown U.
Aarushi Sahejpal is a data science and AI professor at American University and data editor at the Investigative Reporting Workshop.

Aaron Sankin is the data editor at The Marshall Project, a nonprofit news organization covering the criminal justice system in America. He was previously an investigative reporter at The Markup and the Center for Investigative Reporting.

Piotr is an Assistant Research Professor at Northeastern University. His work focuses on experimental verification of online platforms and their algorithms, with the focus on discrimination, privacy violations and disregard for user agency. He has collaborated with reporters at ProPublica, The Markup, The Atlantic and The New York Times. He has served as an expert for the Department of Justice in civil rights cases, and audited platforms with NGOs including Amnesty International.

Tiasia Saunders is a Howard Center for Investigative Journalism Data Journalist Fellow at the Philip Merrill College of Journalism at the University of Maryland in College Park, MD. Additionally, she is a graduate student studying data journalism and is expected to graduate in May 2026. Lastly, she will be a Data Journalist in the Data Bureau in the Spring 2026 semester for Capital News Service.

Daniel has worked in the Information Security Space for over 25 years, and is currently Head of Investigations at DomainTools. He has a background in Cyber Threat Hunting and Incident Response, and has worked in Government and Higher Education before joining the private sector. His first "real" job however was as a Stringer for the regional newspaper "Suedwest Presse" in southern Germany, where in the 1990s he covered community interest stories of the region.

Ivy Scott is an engagement reporter for The Marshall Project, responsible for producing community-driven, investigative and public service journalism about the criminal justice systems in St. Louis and Missouri. She was previously a beat reporter at The Boston Globe, covering climate solutions, state courts, police accountability and the Massachusetts Department of Correction. Her work has been honored as a Livingston finalist and Webby nominee.

Libby Seline is a University of Chicago graduate student studying computational analysis and public policy. Before grad school, she was a data reporter at the San Antonio Express-News via the Texas Development Hub, and then she worked at the Flatwater Free Press as their first election guide editor. She also has bylines in The New York Times, the Salt Lake Tribune and the Lincoln Journal Star.

Ryan Serpico is deputy director of newsroom AI and automation at Hearst Newspapers' DevHub, where he builds AI-powered tools that help journalists find stories and serve readers. His work includes Assembly/Meeting Monitor, winner of the 2024 Online Journalism Award, and Producer-P, used 1,000+ times monthly across Hearst properties. A University of Florida journalism graduate, he's focused on technology that amplifies reporters, not replaces them.

speakers.affiliation: Nieman Foundation for Journalism at Harvard University
speakers.bio: Sotiris Sideris is a 2026 Nieman Fellow at Harvard and an investigative data journalist, editor and educator working on cross-border accountability investigations at the intersection of technology, power and the public interest. His work develops and scrutinizes data-driven and AI-assisted methods to expose systemic abuses and opaque institutions. He collaborates with international newsrooms and trains journalists in data reporting, OSINT and responsible AI.

Ellis is a data reporter at ProPublica, where his work pairs quantitative analysis with traditional investigative reporting techniques. He often covers stories at the intersection of money and power. His recent work has interrogated the ways that nonprofit organizations raise and spend their money. Simani has also investigated the stock trading of the wealthiest Americans. Before joining ProPublica in 2019, he worked on the Los Angeles Times’ data visualization desk.

speakers.affiliation: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP)

speakers.bio: Casey Smith is a senior reporter at the Indiana Capital Chronicle, part of States Newsroom. She previously reported on the Indiana Legislature for The Associated Press. She holds degrees from UC Berkeley’s Graduate School of Journalism and Ball State University. Smith's reporting has earned the Goldsmith Prize, George Polk Award, Selden Ring, and recognition as a Pulitzer Prize finalist, among other honors. Indiana's SPJ Chapter named her journalist of the year in 2025.

Drew Solt is a second-year graduate student at West Virginia University. He is set to graduate with a Master's Degree in Journalism in May. Solt graduated in May of 2024 from WVU with a bachelor's degree in Journalism. He works as a graduate student at WVU Health Sciences Marketing and Communications and freelances in live sports production for WVU Athletics.

Paroma Soni is a data and graphics reporter at POLITICO covering trade, agriculture, politics and immigration. She produces data-driven visual analyses for POLITICO Pro on topics such as the Trump administration’s tariffs, federal funding, visa policy and shifts in domestic manufacturing. Previously, she was an associate visual journalist at FiveThirtyEight covering elections and politics, and a Delacorte Fellow at Columbia Journalism Review. She is based in New York.

Stephanie Stamm is an award-winning visual editor for The Wall Street Journal, specializing in data visualization, analysis and illustration. Before joining the Journal, she worked for the Washington Post and National Journal. For fun, she watercolors and plays video games.

Sam Stecklow is an investigative journalist and FOIA fellow with Invisible Institute, a nonprofit public accountability journalism organization based in Chicago. He works on investigations into police misconduct and abuse, and on II's public data tools the Civic Police Data Project and the National Police Index. His work has been recognized with a Sunshine Award from Utah SPJ and a Driehaus Award for investigative journalism. He is currently based in Orlando.

Matt Stiles is a senior data and graphics editor for CNN Digital's Visual News team. In his role, he helps lead a team of visual journalists around the world and is responsible for anticipating and executing data-driven visual coverage.

Stephen Stock is Assistant News Director and Investigative Journalist at WBRZ Baton Rouge. He specializes in public corruption, safety and transportation. Stock was a founding member of CBS News and Stations Innovation Lab and helped build investigative teams in San Francisco, Miami and Orlando. He teaches at IRE, newsrooms and universities around the US. He’s won a Peabody, a du-Pont, a national SPJ, 3 Murrows, 6 AP awards and 18 Emmys.

Stephanie is the managing reporter for the U.S. Press Freedom Tracker, where she oversees the newsroom and contributes to its coverage of press freedom violations across the United States. A graduate of New York University’s Global and Joint Program Studies program in journalism and international relations, her professional work focuses on human rights, politics and identity-targeted violence.

Samantha Sunne is the co-author of the textbook “Data + Journalism: A Story-Driven Approach to Learning Data Reporting,” now being published in its second edition. She is a freelance journalist based in New Orleans, specializing in data and investigative techniques. Sunne teaches at conferences, universities and newsrooms around the world.
Andrea Suozzo is a news apps developer at ProPublica, where she runs Nonprofit Explorer and does data reporting on nonprofits, maternal health and other topics.
Aadit Tambe is a news designer at The Washington Post, where he builds interactive stories to explain the news visually.

Carson TerBush is a visual journalist and graphics reporter based in Washington, D.C. She most recently worked as a designer and developer at The Washington Post, specializing in data-driven interactives.

Mago Torres is an investigative data journalist and editor with experience working at the intersection of research and data. She has worked on award-winning long-form investigations and developed programs that support collaborations, data-driven stories and investigative journalism in the U.S. and internationally. She is currently a trainer for North America for the Google News Initiative.

Diara J. Townes is an award-winning environmental engagement journalist from New York. She's covered stories shaped by state and national trends through freelance reporting and her newsletter, The Curious Scout. She’s NC Local's program manager, supporting their expanding programmatic work for North Carolina journalists. Diara has worked for Aspen Digital and Sunlight Research Center and has served as an adjunct at the Newmark Graduate School of Journalism at CUNY.

Daniel Trielli is an assistant professor of media and democracy at the University of Maryland's Philip Merrill College of Journalism. He researches the impact of algorithmic systems on the distribution and production of journalism. He is interested in data and computational journalism, media literacy and algorithmic accountability. He occasionally writes about it in public-facing media, both in the United States and his native Brazil.

Kevin Uhrmacher is the deputy news applications editor at ProPublica, where he works with a team of developers to conceive and publish impactful data-driven journalism. Before joining ProPublica, he spent more than 11 years at The Washington Post as a graphics assignment editor and reporter focused on politics and elections.

Jodi Upton is the Knight Chair at Syracuse University, teaching investigative/data journalism classes. She is a consultant for the Knight Commission and the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Committee. Her research includes college athletics’ spending and gender inequities in college sports, police misconduct in New York and mass killings. A transparency advocate, Upton and her students have filed more than 1,000 open records requests in recent years.
Kyle Vass is an investigative journalist who runs a not for profit news site (Dragline) in West Virginia. His work focuses on government accountability with an emphasis on police misconduct.

Matt Waite is a journalism professor at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln who teaches courses in AI, data analysis and visualization. From 2007-11, he was a programmer/journalist for the St. Petersburg Times, where he developed PolitiFact, winner of the 2009 Pulitzer Prize for national reporting. Before that, he was an award-winning investigative reporter and co-author of "Paving Paradise: Florida's Vanishing Wetlands and the Failure of No Net Loss."

Tejal Wakchoure investigates data stories and creates graphics for NBC. Previously, she worked for The Baltimore Banner, where her team supported a Pulitzer Prize-winning investigative series on Baltimore's overdose crisis. She also served on the judging panel for the 2024 IRE Awards. Wakchoure studied data journalism at Columbia University and media analysis at MIT, and majored in computer science at BITS Pilani, India. Wakchoure is based in New York.

Lucia Walinchus is an award-winning journalist, attorney and ice hockey player/coach. She is currently the managing data editor for NBC Owned Stations. She has been featured as a guest speaker on CNN and was a contracted freelancer for The New York Times and The Washington Post. Her work has previously been recognized as the Best Investigative Reporting in Ohio. She is a 2026 winner of the Dupont Columbia award.

Rob Wells, Ph.D., is an associate professor at the Philip Merrill College of Journalism at the University of Maryland, where he teaches reporting and data journalism. Wells’s research interests involve examining the history of business journalism and its problems, such as business journalism’s inability to serve the general public effectively. He is the author of two books.
Ben Welsh is a reporter, an editor and a computer programmer.

Daniel Wheaton is the senior data journalist for the Midwest Newsroom, a collaboration of NPR member stations in Iowa, Kansas, Missouri and Nebraska. He collaborates with station reporters to produce in-depth and investigative journalism and provide data journalism assistance and graphic reporting to the region.

Brian Whitton is a computational journalist at The Wall Street Journal. He builds reporting tools and analyzes data for some of the Journal's most ambitious projects. If the numbers are big, he's probably involved somehow.

Eunjee Wi is a South Korean journalist and a Hubert H. Humphrey Fellow under the Fulbright Program at Arizona State University. She works at The Dong-A Ilbo, one of South Korea’s major national newspapers, where she served on its award-winning investigative unit, the Hero Content Team. She has worked as a project manager for interactive stories based on investigative reporting.
Derek Willis teaches and does journalism, especially data journalism, at the University of Maryland. He also loves cricket.
Cody Winchester was a newspaper reporter, data specialist and web developer before joining IRE as a training director in 2017. He became tech lead in 2022.

Hannah is a data journalist and researcher based in Stanford, California. She most recently contracted as an AI specialist at Reuters, where she previously worked on the data team. At Stanford, she is developing knowledge graphs to structure complex news archives.

Matthew Wuschke is a junior at Boston University studying journalism with an focus on sports and data journalism. He covers athletics for Boston University's student organization WTBU Sports, and has experience in writing, radio, photography and graphic design. He has skills in data extraction and analysis with a interest in Artificial Intelligence and how it can be used effectively in newsrooms.
Leon Yin is an investigative data journalist at Bloomberg News. He covers technology.

Emily Zentner is the data journalist for the statewide public and nonprofit media collaborative The California Newsroom, where she works with partner newsrooms on investigative stories and trains reporters to use data skills in their reporting. Her work primarily focuses on police accountability and climate. She was previously the data reporter at CapRadio in Sacramento, where she reported on wildfire, climate change and police mishandling of sexual assault cases.

Kayla Zhu is the public relations specialist at Visualping, an AI-powered website change detection tool, where she manages the company’s Journalist Program. She has previously worked in data journalism roles for various online media outlets.

Lucy Zimmerman is a master's student in computer science at Stanford University focused on technology for social impact. She has conducted research on the societal impacts of generative AI at Stanford HAI and contributed to projects spanning computational journalism, civic tech and human-centered machine learning. Lucy is passionate about building tools that strengthen democratic institutions and expand access to critical information.

Jerry Zremski is the Klingenstein Family Endowed Chair in Journalism and director of the Local News Network at the University of Maryland's Philip Merrill College of Journalism. He joined Merrill College full-time in 2022 after serving as an adjunct professor for 10 years. A former Nieman fellow at Harvard University, Zremski previously was the longtime Washington bureau chief for The Buffalo News.

McMillan recently joined ProPublica as a computational journalist. Previously, he was a data journalist on NPR’s investigations team, where he combined reporting with data analysis, building tools that transformed raw records into evidence for accountability reporting.

Lizzy Alspach is a senior journalism and Spanish major at the University of Maryland. There, she guided data analysis in the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism’s "Off the Rails" project, which revealed how hazardous materials on trains face minimal regulations. She’s also editor in chief of The Diamondback, a university independent student newspaper.

Daranee Balachandar graduated from the University of Maryland with a masters in journalism, focused on investigative and data reporting. She interned with the Investigations team at The Washington Post in 2025. During her time at Maryland, she served as a reporter for Capital News Service and the Howard Center for Investigative Journalism, and as a data journalist for The Diamondback, the university’s student newspaper.

sponsors.name: Michael I. Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism
sponsors.description: The Michael I. Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism is dedicated to educating students in investigative reporting techniques and creating provocative, original journalism for the public in Indiana and beyond. As a working newsroom, it strives to protect the vulnerable, expose wrongdoing, and act as a watchdog in Indiana and nationally. The center’s work is available at no cost to local, regional and national news outlets and seeks to supplement their reporting at a time when many are losing newsroom staff.

sponsors.name: Bright Data
sponsors.description:
Bright Data is the leading web data infrastructure company, trusted by over 20,000 organizations to ethically access and collect public web data at scale. Bright Data’s platform supports teams across AI, finance, e-commerce, travel, marketing and other markets with the performance, reliability, and compliance standards needed for large-scale data operations.
 
Built for large-scale use, the platform integrates seamlessly into existing systems and data stacks, offering full control over collection workflows. With built-in governance, flexible delivery, and alignment to global compliance standards, it supports secure, efficient, and auditable operations.

sponsors.name: Knight Foundation
sponsors.description: We are social investors who support a more effective democracy by funding free expression and journalism, arts and culture in community, research in areas of media and democracy, and the success of American cities and towns where the Knight brothers once published newspapers.

sponsors.name: Lumina Foundation
sponsors.description: Lumina Foundation is an independent, private foundation in Indianapolis committed to making opportunities for learning beyond high school available to all. We envision higher learning that is easy to navigate, delivers fair results, and meets the nation’s talent needs through a broad range of credentials. We work toward a system that prepares people for informed citizenship and success in a global economy.

sponsors.name: The Media School at Indiana University
sponsors.description: The Media School combines a liberal arts education with hands-on, professional training to propel students into the world as leaders in the media industry. We offer our students extensive, early opportunities and cutting-edge facilities and equipment to foster a creative community both inside and outside the classroom.

sponsors.name: ProPublica
sponsors.description: ProPublica is an independent, nonprofit newsroom that produces investigative journalism with moral force. With a team of more than 150 dedicated journalists, we focus on stories with the potential to spur real-world impact. We dig deep into important issues, shining a light on abuses of power and betrayals of public trust — and we stick with those issues as long as it takes to hold power to account.

sponsors.name: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press
sponsors.description: The Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press (RCFP) is the leading pro bono legal services organization for journalists and newsrooms in the U.S. The Reporters Committee provides legal representation, amicus curiae support, and other legal resources at no cost to protect First Amendment freedoms and the newsgathering rights of journalists across the country.

sponsors.name: University of Missouri School of Journalism

exhibitors.name: Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism/ The Media School at Indiana University
exhibitors.description:
The Michael I. Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism is dedicated to educating students in investigative reporting techniques and creating provocative, original journalism for the public in Indiana and beyond. 
The Media School combines a liberal arts education with hands-on, professional training to propel students into the world as leaders in the media industry. We offer our students extensive, early opportunities and cutting-edge facilities and equipment to foster a creative community both inside and outside the classroom.

exhibitors.name: Data-Driven Reporting Project | Knight Lab
exhibitors.description: The Data-Driven Reporting Project (DDRP) aims to support news journalists and freelancers working on document-based investigative projects that serve local and/or underrepresented communities in the US and Canada. Based in the Knight Lab at Northwestern, the DDRP has awarded more than $2.2 million to 59 news organizations since 2022.

exhibitors.name: Everlaw for Good
exhibitors.description: Everlaw's document review and discovery platform, free for journalists and newsrooms, helps reporters dig deeper into data, surface insights, and streamline their investigations to chart a straighter path to the truth. Learn more at: https://www.everlaw.com/forgood/

exhibitors.name: IndyStar/USA Today Co.
exhibitors.description: IndyStar is part of the USA TODAY Network, the largest local-to-national publishing and digital media organization in the country. USA TODAY, our national flagship newsroom, sits at the center of the network, surrounded by hundreds of local newsrooms like IndyStar reporting on the stories and cultural moments happening in our communities.

exhibitors.name: News Media Help Desk
exhibitors.description: News Media Help Desk is an online resource designed to empower journalists and other roles that support news operations to make meaningful and strategic decisions that lead to the sustainability of local media.

exhibitors.name: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press
SciLine
exhibitors.description:
SciLine is a service for journalists and scientists based at the American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS), the world’s largest multidisciplinary scientific society. Editorially independent, nonpartisan, and funded by philanthropies, SciLine has the singular mission of enhancing the amount and quality of scientific evidence in news stories.

SciLine works directly with U.S. journalists to support the incorporation of scientific evidence and expertise into their news stories. We facilitate one-on-one interviews with scientists, host media briefings on science topics, collect and distribute expert quotes on breaking news, and organize professional training events—with a special emphasis on the needs of local and general assignment reporters. All services for journalists are free.

We also work directly with U.S. and Canadian scientists, recruiting them to serve as experts in all of our services for journalists as well as offering training to prepare them for successful interactions with the media.

exhibitors.name: University of Florida Brechner FOI Project
exhibitors.description: The Joseph L. Brechner Freedom of Information Project at the University of Florida College of Journalism and Communications has, since 1977, provided research, education and assistance for journalists regarding access to government information. We provide research-based facts about the state of transparency in the states and federally, training for newsrooms, and guidance and quotes for journalists covering public records. Our Secrecy Tracker monitors FOI-related legislation in all the states, and we coordinate national Sunshine Week. We provide a free monthly email newsletter, called The Brechner Report, about the latest news, research and tips pertaining to freedom of information (subscribe!). Our table at NICAR will be staffed by Director David Cuillier and Secrecy Tracker coordinator Samantha Sunne, both experienced trainers and practitioners in data journalism.

exhibitors.name: University of Illinois - Department of Journalism
exhibitors.description:
As a Top 10 Public University*, the Department of Journalism at Illinois offers a curriculum that will prepare you for print, broadcast, multimedia, public affairs reporting, and more. In small lab courses, students gain hands-on experiences working with state-of-the-art technology to gather, analyze, and present news and features. The Richard and Leslie Frank Newsroom offers a real-world television environment at Richmond Studio. Students can also explore their interests in politics, social justice, law, science, technology, or sports through specialized journalism courses. 
*US News & World Report
